content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
David P. Hale 
A Professional Corporation 
City Attorney 
Cities of Grover Beach and Tulare 
401 Clovis Ave., Ste. 208 
Clovis, CA. 93612 
April 10, 2017 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-17-064 
Dear Mr. Hale: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Grover Beach 
Councilmember Debbie Peterson regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that this advice does not analyze any other area of law, 
including Section 1090, the Public Contract Code, or common law conflicts of interest. Please also 
note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), 
and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Because your questions 
are general in nature and not limited to a specific pending decision, we are treating your request as 
one for informal assistance.2 
QUESTION 
Do the conflict of interest provisions under the Act prohibit Councilmember Peterson from 
making or participating in making governmental decisions concerning cannabis-related ordinances 
for Grover Beach? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. As explained below, Councilmember Peterson has a disqualifying conflict of interest in 
governmental decisions concerning cannabis-related ordinances for Grover Beach. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
? Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) 


FACTS 
FileNo. I-17-064 
Page No. 2 
The city council is currently adopting several land use and regulatory ordinances related to 
the marijuana industry, which are designed to allow the use of medical marijuana within the city. 
Councilmember Peterson is a real tor who will be potentially working with both sellers and buyers 
in the acquisition or sale of properties subject to these ordinances. She has been licensed as a real 
estate broker since 1999, and has lived, worked, and maintained her office in the City of Grover 
Beach during that period. 
Grover Beach voters recently voted to tax cannabis sales in the city. Councilmember 
Peterson will now be involved in her official capacity in creating land use, tax and regulatory 
ordinances for the cannabis industry. These ordinances may affect real estate values and 
availability, and are intended to generate tax revenues for the city as well as economic vitality for 
the community. 
As a licensed realtor broker for Peterson Team Realty,3 Councilmember Peterson stands to 
benefit from her specific expertise in Grover Beach. She is also uniquely qualified to work with 
business and commercial properties because prior to her real estate career, she was a consultant to 
several redevelopment companies and small industrial and retail businesses owners, and was the 
owner and founder of several retail, service and manufacturing companies. Currently, she is getting 
inquiries from existing and potential clients who are considering buying, selling, or leasing real 
estate in Grover Beach because it will be the first city in the county to allow cannabis businesses. 
Property prices are escalating significantly and Councilmember Peterson expects it is only a matter 
of time before one of the inquiries results in a transaction that will generate a commission. 
There are currently 23 properties on the market in the areas allowing cannabis businesses, 
and likely double that number of real tors active in the cannabis zone. The multiple listing service 
shows 75 real estate agents listed in Grover Beach and 2,014 realtors licensed to work in the city. 
Councilmember Peterson spends more than 30 hours a week on council matters, and earns 
only $300 per month, with no benefits or expenses, as a councilmember. She therefore relies almost 
entirely on commissions from her work as a broker to support herself. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's 
interests set forth in Section 87103 as follows: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
3 We assume Councilmember Peterson is the sole owner of Peterson Team Realty. 


File No. 1-17-064 
Page No. 3 
• 
Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b ).) 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103{d).) 
• 
Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $470 or 
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months 
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
Under your facts, Councilmember Peterson would have an interest in Peterson Team Realty 
as a business entity. And pursuant to Regulation 18700.1, an official who receives commission 
income4 as a broker, agent, or salesperson for a specific sale5 or similar transaction may have 
interests in both the person the agent represents and the business entity under whose auspices he or 
she works. (Regulation 18700.1 (c).) Thus, both Peterson Team Realty and any other person from 
whom Councilmember Peterson receives commission income are a source of income to her if her 
commission income from that person aggregates to $500 or more 12 months prior to a decision. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Initially, we note that no official has a conflict of interest merely by being a real estate 
professional. (Regulation 18701.1.) Rather, a conflict of interest exists only if the decision in 
question will foreseeably and materially affect an official's interests. 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability 
differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Financial interests 
that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party in, or subject of, a government 
decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) Based on the facts, none of Councilmember Peterson's potential 
interests are explicitly involved in the governmental decisions. For interests that are not explicitly 
involved, as here, the effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. "In general, 
if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." (Regulation 18701(b).) 
4 "Commission income" means gross payments received by a public official as a result of services rendered as 
a broker, agent, or other salesperson for a specific sale or similar transaction. Commission income is received when it is 
paid or credited. 
5 "Promised income," within the meaning of Section 87103(c), may also be a disqualifying financial interest. 
We have advised that commission income is deemed promised income to a real estate broker when a sale is pending 
(i.e. the property is in escrow) - having a listing on a property does not constitute promised income. Therefore, 
Councilmember Peterson will not have an interest in existing or future clients where there is no pending sale. 


File No. 1-17-064 
Page No. 4 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's business interest is 
material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision s effect would contribute to a change in the value of the privately-held business entity. 
That regulation also provides various examples of the types of decisions that may impact the 
analysis, including the example of decisions that would "increase or d»crease the need for the 
products or services that the business entity supplies." (Regulation 18702. 1 (b )(3 ). ) 
According to the facts provided, the city council, including Councilmember Peterson, will 
be creating land use, tax and regulatory ordinances as a result of a new law to tax cannabis sales in 
the city. You state the ordinances may impact real estate values and availability, and that property 
prices are escalating significantly, presumably in anticipation of the new ordinances. By your own 
concession, there is a realistic possibility that the new cannabis-related ordinances will increase the 
demand for Councilmember Peterson's brokerage services to buy, sell and lease real estate in 
Grover Beach- all of which should positively impact the value of her real estate business. Indeed, 
Councilmember Peterson is already getting inquiries from existing and potential clients because 
Grover Beach will be the first city in the county to allow cannabis businesses. And it is likely safe 
to assume that the increased interest in real estate will continue as a result of the new ordinances. 
Councilmember Peterson will therefore have a disqualifying conflict of interest in decisions 
involving cannabis-related ordinances that will likely have the effect of increasing the demand for 
her real estate services. 
When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (such as a city 
councilmember) has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she 
must: (I) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of interest 
involved in the decision as well as details of the interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse 
himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. 
(Section 87105; Regulation 18707.) 
Exceptions 
I. Public Generally 
Section 87103 prohibits an official from taking part in a decision only if the effect of the 
decision on the official's interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. 
Commonly referred to as the "public generally" exception, Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the 
general rule: 
"A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's 
financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally if the official establishes that a significant segment of the 
public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not 
unique compared to the effect on the significant segment." 


FileNo. 1-17-064 
Page No. 5 
For purposes of the "public generally" exception (as applicable to your facts), a "significant 
segment of the public" consists of at least 25 percent of the businesses in the official's jurisdiction. 6 
(Regulation 18703{b).) Here, you have provided no facts to suggest that the new cannabis-related 
ordinances will affect at least 25 percent of all businesses in Grover Beach in the same manner as 
your real estate business. Therefore, the public generally exception would not apply.7 
2. Communications with the Press or Public. 
A public official is not attempting to use his or her official position to influence a 
governmental decision of an agency if the official communicates with the general public or the 
press. (Regulation 18702.4{b)(2).) For instance, we have previously advised that a city 
councilmember may express his or her opinion to reporters and media outlets by writing a 
newspaper article or appearing on a radio program regarding the matter in which he or she has a 
conflict of interest under the Act. (Edelen Advice Letter, No. 1-07-059; Acker Advice Letter, No.A-
01-117.) 
3. Appearance as a Member of the General Public. 
An official is not making, participating in making or influencing a governmental decision if 
he or she makes an appearance "as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of 
its prescribed governmental function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the 
official's personal interest . ... " (Regulation l 8704(d)(2).) The exception applies to a business 
"owned entirely by the official, members of his or her immediate family, or the official and 
members of his or her immediate family." (Regulation l 8704(d)(2)(B).) 
This exception is interpreted narrowly and does not allow an official to provide general 
comments regarding the matter. Care should be taken to clarify that the official is not acting in an 
official capacity. (McHugh Advice Letter, No. I-98-324.) Thus, the exception does not permit you 
to represent any other person's interests or the interests of any group. To qualify for the exception, 
an official must follow the procedures set forth in Regulation 18707, explained above. 
6 For purposes of the exception. an official's "jurisdiction" is the geographical area in which the official's 
agency has jurisdiction or the geographical area the official was elected to represent. (Section 82035; Regulation 
18703(d).) 
7 If there is a specific cannabis-related decision that comes before the city council where the facts suggest it 
may affect 25 percent or more of all business in Grover Beach in a similar manner to your business, we recommend you 
request written advice from the Commission prior to the decision to determine whether this exception applies. 


JW:jgl 
File No. I-17-064 
Page No. 6 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
Seni r Counsel, Legal Division

for Grover Beach?

making or participating in making governmental decisions concerning cannabis-related ordinances 
for Grover Beach?

CONCLUSION: Yes. As explained below, Councilmember Peterson has a disqualifying conflict of interest in 
governmental decisions concerning cannabis-related ordinances for Grover Beach.? Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).)
